comments.text:
As an additional remark, if we consider the conditions of Problem #973, one can also derive the following partial result. From Theorem 6.1 (the First Main Theorem) of [Tu84], if $\min_i|z_i|=1$ (hence $|z_i|\ge1$) and $g(
u)=\sum_{i=1}^n b_i z_i^
u$, then for any $m\ge0$\[ \max_{
u=m+1,\dots,m+n} |g(
u)| \;\ge\; \left( rac{n}{2e(m+n)}ight)^{\!n}\,\left|\sum_{i=1}^n b_iight|. \]Taking $b_i\equiv1$ and $m=1$ (so $
u=2,\dots,n+1$) yields\[ \max_{2\le k\le n+1}\left|\sum_{i=1}^n z_i^kight| \;\ge\; n\left( rac{n}{2e(n+1)}ight)^{\!n}. \]Therefore, if an exponential upper bound $C^{-n}$ exists in the $|z_i|\ge1$ setting of Problem #973, one must have $C\le 2e+o(1)$. Reference. [Tu84] P. Turán, On a New Method of Analysis and Its Applications, John Wiley & Sons, 1984. (The site has been updated to address this comment.)

The page mentions ''Erdős proved (but I cannot find the reference) that such a sequence does exist with $|z_i|\le1$.'' I believe I have found the missing reference. The result appears as Theorem 4.1 on page 35 of [Tu84]. In that theorem Turán attributes the result to Erdős, proving that for\[ S_2=\{2,3,\dots,n,n+1\}, \qquad M_2=\min_{\{z_j\}}\max_{\nu\in S_2}\left|\sum_{j=1}^n z_j^{\nu}\right|, \]under the normalization $\max_j|z_j|=1$, one has\[ M_2\le 2(n+1)^2 e^{-\vartheta_0 n} < 1.32^{-n} \qquad (n>n_0), \]where $\vartheta_0\approx0.2784$ satisfies $x e^{x+1}=1$. EDIT: Furthermore, in [Er92] Erdős refines the result and provides a more detailed asymptotic estimation for \(M_2\), showing that for sufficiently large \(n\),\[ n^{-0.7823} e^{-2\vartheta_0 n} \leq M_2 \leq n^{4.5} e^{-2\vartheta_0 n}. \]For \(n > n_0\), we also have the following bounds for \(M_2\):\[ \frac{1}{1.746^n} < M_2 < \frac{1}{1.745^n}.\]References. [Tu84] Paul Turán, On a New Method of Analysis and Its Applications, published posthumously with the assistance of Gábor Halász and János Pintz, John Wiley & Sons, New York, 1984 (Pure and Applied Mathematics series). [Er92] L. Erdős, "On Some Problems of P. Turán Concerning Power Sums of Complex Numbers," Acta Mathematica Hungarica, 59.1 (1992): 11-24. (The site has been updated to address this comment.)

Interestingly, as observed in Erdos's paper, the answer is completely different if one replaces the range $2 \leq k \leq n+1$ by $1 \leq k \leq n$. Here one has the example of the $(n+1)^{th}$ roots of unity (if $n+1$ is prime) to show that the max can be 1, but it was shown by Atkinson that it must be $\gg 1$ (and possibly even $\geq 1/2$)! Turan conjectures a lower bound of $1-o(1)$ as $n \to \infty$. It is not clear whether there is a similar construction showing that $1$ can be attained (or beaten) in the $2 \leq k \leq n+1$ case. EDIT: Here is a summary of Atkinson's argument. Suppose for contradiction that the power sums $s_k = \sum_{1 \leq i \leq n} z_i^k$ are all small for $1 \leq k \leq n$. The function $g(\theta) = -\sum_{k=1}^n \frac{s_k e(k\theta)}{k}$ is the Fourier expansion of $\sum_{1 \leq i \leq n} \log(1-z_i e(\theta))$ up to degree $n$, hence the Fourier expansion of $e^{g(\theta)}$ agrees with $\prod_{1 \leq i \leq n} (1-z_i e(\theta))$ up to degree $n$. If we set $z_1=1$, we conclude that at $\theta=0$, $e^{g(0)}$ is equal to the tail of its Fourier expansion after degree $n$; thus $e^{g(0)}$ is formally equal to the inner product of $e^{g(\theta)}$ and $\sum_{m=n+1}^\infty e(m\theta)$, or on integrating by parts of $e^{g(\theta)} g'(\theta)$ and $-\sum_{m=n+1}^\infty e(m\theta)/2\pi i -m$. Thus the inner product of $e^{g(\theta)-g(0)} g'(\theta)$ and $-\sum_{m=n+1}^\infty e(m\theta)/2\pi i m$ is equal to $1$. But using Plancherel's theorem to control the derivative of $g'$, one can show by standard estimates that this inner product is small if the $s_k$ are all small, giving the result. More informally: $e^{g(\theta)-g(0)}$ has significant presence at high frequencies, which is why $g'$ cannot have all of its Fourier coefficients small.
[Post deleted, as the problem statement has been updated.] (The site has been updated to address this comment.)

On reading the original paper of Erdos it appears that the quantifiers in the formulation got permuted by mistake. One should ask whether there is a C such that for every n there is a finite sequence x, rather than ask for an infinite sequence that works for all n and some C. (The site has been updated to address this comment.)
Yes, apologies, this was the intended question of Erdős; I'll fix it now.
